title: Salivary Gland Disease (Sialadenitis, Stones, Tumours)
oneLiner: Inflammatory, obstructive or neoplastic salivary disorders; presents with pain/swelling; risks include sepsis, airway compromise, and missed malignancy.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction** from deep neck space infection (e.g., Ludwig’s angina secondary to submandibular sialadenitis)  
  - *Single finding:* Stridor, drooling, or inability to swallow own secretions
- **Sepsis** from acute suppurative sialadenitis (most commonly parotid)  
  - *Single finding:* Fever >38.5°C + unilateral tender erythematous gland with purulent duct discharge
- **Necrotising sialometaplasia** (ischaemic mimic of malignancy) – rare but can cause rapid tissue destruction  
  - *Single finding:* Painful, ulcerated, necrotic mass in palate or parotid + history of radiotherapy
sections.Presentation:
- **Sialadenitis:**  
  - Unilateral gland pain, swelling, worse with eating  
  - Fever, trismus, erythema over gland  
  - Purulent saliva from duct (milking the gland)
- **Sialolithiasis (stones):**  
  - Intermittent colicky pain and swelling before or during meals  
  - Stone palpable in duct (submandibular most common – Wharton’s duct)  
  - Post-prandial relief after swelling subsides
- **Tumours:**  
  - Painless, slow-growing, firm or hard lump (parotid – pre-auricular; submandibular – submental)  
  - Facial nerve palsy (parotid tumour – sinister sign for malignancy)  
  - Fixation to skin/deep tissues, cervical lymphadenopathy
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute sialadenitis vs. lymphadenitis** – gland swelling with duct purulence points to salivary cause; lymph node is discrete, not anatomically glandular
- **Sialolithiasis vs. salivary tumour** – pain intermittent with meals is stone; constant swelling ± facial nerve involvement is tumour
- **Parotid tumour vs. parotid abscess** – abscess: hot, tender, fluctuant, fever; tumour: cold, non-tender, firm, ± facial nerve signs
- **Splitter:** *Milking the duct – purulent discharge confirms sialadenitis; absence of discharge but palpable stone in duct suggests sialolithiasis*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Milking the gland – observe duct orifice for purulence or stone  
  - Simple palpation of floor of mouth (Wharton’s duct) and cheek (Stensen’s duct)
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (WCC, CRP), U&Es, amylase (if pancreatitis suspected – salivary amylase can be elevated in mumps)  
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**  
  - *First-line:* Ultrasound (US) neck – identifies stones, abscess, gland swelling, cystic or solid mass  
  - *Second-line:* Non-contrast CT head/neck (if stone suspected but US negative or suspected deep neck infection)  
  - *Tumour workup:* MRI neck with contrast ± FNAC or core biopsy  
  - *Sialography* – rarely used now; MR sialography may help
- **Special tests:**  
  - *FNAC* (fine-needle aspiration cytology) for any solid salivary lump – sensitivity ~90% for malignancy  
  - *Mumps serology* (IgM) if bilateral parotitis with systemic features  
  - *Autoantibodies* (anti-Ro/La) – Sjögren’s syndrome if recurrent, dry mouth/eyes
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Lactate + venous blood gas** if septic/sick – guides early antibiotics and resuscitation
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If airway compromise – call anaesthetics ± ENT senior for emergency decompression  
  - Sepsis: take blood cultures, start IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2 g TDS) after discussion with microbiology  
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1 g IV/PO + ibuprofen 400 mg PO (if no contraindication)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - **Sialadenitis/abscess:**  
    - IV antibiotics (community: co-amoxiclav; if penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600 mg QDS IV)  
    - Warm compresses, gland massage  
    - Sialogogues (lemon drops, sour candy) to promote salivary flow  
    - If abscess: request ENT review for US-guided aspiration or incision/drainage  
  - **Sialolithiasis:**  
    - Analgesia, hydration, sialogogues  
    - Small stones (<5 mm): 80% pass spontaneously; encourage massage, warm drinks  
    - Failure: refer to ENT for sialendoscopy / lithotripsy / duct dilation  
  - **Tumour:**  
    - No acute medical intervention – refer to ENT or OMFS for biopsy and staging  
    - If aetiology unclear (e.g., first presentation of painless lump) – arrange US + FNAC as outpatient
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Recurrent sialadenitis: consider sialendoscopy, duct dilation, or gland excision  
  - Tumours: surgical excision (superficial parotidectomy, submandibulectomy) ± radiotherapy  
  - Sjögren’s related: hydroxychloroquine, artificial saliva, ophthalmology review
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline 1 L/8h if NBM, or encourage oral hydration  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless contraindicated)  
  - Diet: soft, non-acidic, encourage sour sweets (sialogogues) if no NBM order  
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, if sepsis – 4-hourly; stable – once daily
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line antibiotics (acute suppurative sialadenitis, community):**  
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS *or* 625 mg PO TDS (if mild)  
  - Duration: 7 days (IV then oral switch when afebrile 48h)  
- **Penicillin allergy (non-anaphylaxis):** clindamycin 600 mg IV QDS *or* 300–450 mg PO QDS  
- **Analgesia:** paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS + ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (max 1.2 g/day)  
- **Sialogogues:** 1–2 drops of lemon juice on tongue or sour sweet – not a drug, but documented  
- **Key contraindications:**  
  - Co-amoxiclav – caution in jaundice history; avoid if mononucleosis suspected  
  - Ibuprofen – avoid if CKD, GI bleed, or on anticoagulants  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Co-amoxiclav – reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min ([check local policy])  
  - Clindamycin – no dose adjustment in renal failure, but caution in severe hepatic impairment  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Gentamicin** (often added if severe sepsis) – must check levels pre-second dose; risk of nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity – avoid unless discussed with microbiology
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Airway obstruction (deep neck infection, Ludwig’s angina)  
  - Sepsis / septic shock  
  - Abscess formation requiring surgical drainage  
  - Facial nerve palsy (parotid malignancy or iatrogenic during surgery)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Recurrent sialadenitis with gland fibrosis  
  - Xerostomia (radiation, Sjögren’s)  
  - Malignant transformation of pleomorphic adenoma (rare, long-term)
sections.Prognosis:
- Acute sialadenitis: resolves in 7–14 days with antibiotics ± drainage; recurrence risk ~20%  
- Sialolithiasis: small stones pass spontaneously; larger stones require intervention – recurrence ~10%  
- Salivary gland tumours: benign (80%) – excellent with complete excision; malignant – 5-year survival varies (mucoepidermoid ~85%, adenoid cystic ~70%)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Sialogogues early** – promote salivation helps flush stones and reduces bacterial load in sialadenitis  
- **Always examine the facial nerve** in any parotid lump – document function before any intervention  
- **Ultrasound + FNAC** is the gold standard first-line workup for any solid salivary lump – arrange before referral
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t assume bilateral parotitis is mumps** – consider Sjögren’s, sarcoidosis, sialolithiasis  
- **Don’t give steroids for a parotid lump without imaging** – can mask diagnosis or cause tumour necrosis  
- **Don’t delay airway assessment** if trismus, stridor, or drooling – call senior immediately
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening pain, swelling, fever >38°C, inability to open mouth  
  - Signs of sepsis (rigors, rapid breathing, confusion)  
  - Sudden onset of facial weakness (nerve involvement)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Sialadenitis: GP in 7 days; refer to ENT if recurrent  
  - Stones: ENT outpatient within 4 weeks if persistent symptoms  
  - Tumour (lump): urgent ENT/OMFS referral within 2 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (ENT/OMFS/ICU) immediately if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >4 mmol/L, or signs of airway compromise  
  - Facial nerve palsy in an acute setting (tumour vs. viral vs. iatrogenic)  
  - Rapidly expanding neck mass with trismus or stridor  
  - Sepsis not responding to IV antibiotics within 2 hours

rawMarkdown:
## Salivary Gland Disease (Sialadenitis, Stones, Tumours)
**One-liner:** Inflammatory, obstructive or neoplastic salivary disorders; presents with pain/swelling; risks include sepsis, airway compromise, and missed malignancy.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction** from deep neck space infection (e.g., Ludwig’s angina secondary to submandibular sialadenitis)  
  - *Single finding:* Stridor, drooling, or inability to swallow own secretions
- **Sepsis** from acute suppurative sialadenitis (most commonly parotid)  
  - *Single finding:* Fever >38.5°C + unilateral tender erythematous gland with purulent duct discharge
- **Necrotising sialometaplasia** (ischaemic mimic of malignancy) – rare but can cause rapid tissue destruction  
  - *Single finding:* Painful, ulcerated, necrotic mass in palate or parotid + history of radiotherapy

### Presentation
- **Sialadenitis:**  
  - Unilateral gland pain, swelling, worse with eating  
  - Fever, trismus, erythema over gland  
  - Purulent saliva from duct (milking the gland)
- **Sialolithiasis (stones):**  
  - Intermittent colicky pain and swelling before or during meals  
  - Stone palpable in duct (submandibular most common – Wharton’s duct)  
  - Post-prandial relief after swelling subsides
- **Tumours:**  
  - Painless, slow-growing, firm or hard lump (parotid – pre-auricular; submandibular – submental)  
  - Facial nerve palsy (parotid tumour – sinister sign for malignancy)  
  - Fixation to skin/deep tissues, cervical lymphadenopathy

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute sialadenitis vs. lymphadenitis** – gland swelling with duct purulence points to salivary cause; lymph node is discrete, not anatomically glandular
- **Sialolithiasis vs. salivary tumour** – pain intermittent with meals is stone; constant swelling ± facial nerve involvement is tumour
- **Parotid tumour vs. parotid abscess** – abscess: hot, tender, fluctuant, fever; tumour: cold, non-tender, firm, ± facial nerve signs
- **Splitter:** *Milking the duct – purulent discharge confirms sialadenitis; absence of discharge but palpable stone in duct suggests sialolithiasis*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Milking the gland – observe duct orifice for purulence or stone  
  - Simple palpation of floor of mouth (Wharton’s duct) and cheek (Stensen’s duct)
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (WCC, CRP), U&Es, amylase (if pancreatitis suspected – salivary amylase can be elevated in mumps)  
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**  
  - *First-line:* Ultrasound (US) neck – identifies stones, abscess, gland swelling, cystic or solid mass  
  - *Second-line:* Non-contrast CT head/neck (if stone suspected but US negative or suspected deep neck infection)  
  - *Tumour workup:* MRI neck with contrast ± FNAC or core biopsy  
  - *Sialography* – rarely used now; MR sialography may help
- **Special tests:**  
  - *FNAC* (fine-needle aspiration cytology) for any solid salivary lump – sensitivity ~90% for malignancy  
  - *Mumps serology* (IgM) if bilateral parotitis with systemic features  
  - *Autoantibodies* (anti-Ro/La) – Sjögren’s syndrome if recurrent, dry mouth/eyes
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Lactate + venous blood gas** if septic/sick – guides early antibiotics and resuscitation

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If airway compromise – call anaesthetics ± ENT senior for emergency decompression  
  - Sepsis: take blood cultures, start IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2 g TDS) after discussion with microbiology  
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1 g IV/PO + ibuprofen 400 mg PO (if no contraindication)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - **Sialadenitis/abscess:**  
    - IV antibiotics (community: co-amoxiclav; if penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600 mg QDS IV)  
    - Warm compresses, gland massage  
    - Sialogogues (lemon drops, sour candy) to promote salivary flow  
    - If abscess: request ENT review for US-guided aspiration or incision/drainage  
  - **Sialolithiasis:**  
    - Analgesia, hydration, sialogogues  
    - Small stones (<5 mm): 80% pass spontaneously; encourage massage, warm drinks  
    - Failure: refer to ENT for sialendoscopy / lithotripsy / duct dilation  
  - **Tumour:**  
    - No acute medical intervention – refer to ENT or OMFS for biopsy and staging  
    - If aetiology unclear (e.g., first presentation of painless lump) – arrange US + FNAC as outpatient
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Recurrent sialadenitis: consider sialendoscopy, duct dilation, or gland excision  
  - Tumours: surgical excision (superficial parotidectomy, submandibulectomy) ± radiotherapy  
  - Sjögren’s related: hydroxychloroquine, artificial saliva, ophthalmology review
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline 1 L/8h if NBM, or encourage oral hydration  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless contraindicated)  
  - Diet: soft, non-acidic, encourage sour sweets (sialogogues) if no NBM order  
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, if sepsis – 4-hourly; stable – once daily

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line antibiotics (acute suppurative sialadenitis, community):**  
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS *or* 625 mg PO TDS (if mild)  
  - Duration: 7 days (IV then oral switch when afebrile 48h)  
- **Penicillin allergy (non-anaphylaxis):** clindamycin 600 mg IV QDS *or* 300–450 mg PO QDS  
- **Analgesia:** paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS + ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (max 1.2 g/day)  
- **Sialogogues:** 1–2 drops of lemon juice on tongue or sour sweet – not a drug, but documented  
- **Key contraindications:**  
  - Co-amoxiclav – caution in jaundice history; avoid if mononucleosis suspected  
  - Ibuprofen – avoid if CKD, GI bleed, or on anticoagulants  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Co-amoxiclav – reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min ([check local policy])  
  - Clindamycin – no dose adjustment in renal failure, but caution in severe hepatic impairment  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Gentamicin** (often added if severe sepsis) – must check levels pre-second dose; risk of nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity – avoid unless discussed with microbiology  

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Airway obstruction (deep neck infection, Ludwig’s angina)  
  - Sepsis / septic shock  
  - Abscess formation requiring surgical drainage  
  - Facial nerve palsy (parotid malignancy or iatrogenic during surgery)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Recurrent sialadenitis with gland fibrosis  
  - Xerostomia (radiation, Sjögren’s)  
  - Malignant transformation of pleomorphic adenoma (rare, long-term)  

### Prognosis
- Acute sialadenitis: resolves in 7–14 days with antibiotics ± drainage; recurrence risk ~20%  
- Sialolithiasis: small stones pass spontaneously; larger stones require intervention – recurrence ~10%  
- Salivary gland tumours: benign (80%) – excellent with complete excision; malignant – 5-year survival varies (mucoepidermoid ~85%, adenoid cystic ~70%)  

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Sialogogues early** – promote salivation helps flush stones and reduces bacterial load in sialadenitis  
- **Always examine the facial nerve** in any parotid lump – document function before any intervention  
- **Ultrasound + FNAC** is the gold standard first-line workup for any solid salivary lump – arrange before referral  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t assume bilateral parotitis is mumps** – consider Sjögren’s, sarcoidosis, sialolithiasis  
- **Don’t give steroids for a parotid lump without imaging** – can mask diagnosis or cause tumour necrosis  
- **Don’t delay airway assessment** if trismus, stridor, or drooling – call senior immediately  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening pain, swelling, fever >38°C, inability to open mouth  
  - Signs of sepsis (rigors, rapid breathing, confusion)  
  - Sudden onset of facial weakness (nerve involvement)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Sialadenitis: GP in 7 days; refer to ENT if recurrent  
  - Stones: ENT outpatient within 4 weeks if persistent symptoms  
  - Tumour (lump): urgent ENT/OMFS referral within 2 weeks  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (ENT/OMFS/ICU) immediately if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >4 mmol/L, or signs of airway compromise  
  - Facial nerve palsy in an acute setting (tumour vs. viral vs. iatrogenic)  
  - Rapidly expanding neck mass with trismus or stridor  
  - Sepsis not responding to IV antibiotics within 2 hours
